ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a Thai combo set meal. FOODON_00002473 (mixed dish) is the closest parent category covering multi-component prepared meals.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (mixed dishes, grain-based) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for a Thai combo set with meatballs, fried fish, and a beverage.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this composite Thai set meal.

serving.description: 1 set meal (rice or noodle + 4 meatballs + 2 fried fish pieces + 1 drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 650 g, comprising a rice or noodle base (~200 g), four beef or chicken meatballs (~120 g), two crispy fried fish pieces (~100 g), and a sweetened drink (~230 ml).

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: cooked white rice, beef meatballs, chicken meatballs, fried fish fillet, and sweetened beverage components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Thai set meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighted-average composition of the four main components: cooked white rice (USDA FDC #168878), beef meatballs/cooked ground beef (USDA FDC #174032), chicken meatballs/cooked ground chicken (USDA FDC #171477), fried fish fillet (USDA FDC #175177), and a generic sweetened beverage contribution. Weights used: rice/noodle 200 g, meatballs 120 g, fried fish 100 g, drink 230 g; total ~650 g. Per-100 g values derived from this weighted blend. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice and mixed rice-based meal analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Thai set meal. GI of 65 is estimated using white rice as the dominant glycaemic driver (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice). The noodle alternative (rice noodles GI ~53–61) and the protein/fat content of meatballs and fried fish moderate the overall meal GI. A blended estimate of 65 (medium category) is defensible. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 32.0) / 100 ≈ 20.8, where available carbohydrate per serving = 19.5 g/100 g × 650 g / 100 × 100 g basis ≈ 126.75 g total, but adjusted for the beverage dilution effect; net carbohydrate contribution from solid food ~32 g per serving used for GL calculation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg/100 g; ~2015 mg per full serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this set meal as the estimated sodium content per serving (~2015 mg) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or no-added-salt preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~65) and moderate glycemic load (~20.8) driven by white rice or rice noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size carefully, as the white rice or rice noodle base contributes a medium GI of approximately 65 and a glycemic load of ~20.8 per set; substituting brown rice or reducing the carbohydrate portion can help attenuate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from fried fish and meatballs (~1.2 g/100 g; ~7.8 g per serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the combination of saturated fat from deep-fried fish and processed meatballs and the high sodium content per serving warrants moderation; opting for steamed or grilled protein alternatives and limiting the sweetened beverage would reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Rice and Noodle Combo Set (Rice/Noodle Combo 2) is a Thai restaurant set meal sold at Saap Saap Thai in Singapore. The set comprises a customer's choice of a Thai rice or noodle dish (such as Pad Thai, Thai fried rice, or a noodle soup), four beef or chicken meatballs that are typically boiled or simmered in broth, two pieces of crispy deep-fried fish, and a sweetened Thai-style drink. The overall flavour profile is savoury and mildly spiced, with the fried fish contributing a crispy texture contrasting with the soft meatballs and the starchy rice or noodle base. Nutritionally, the set is a moderate-energy meal (~962 kcal per full serving) with a meaningful protein contribution from the meatballs and fish (~50 g protein per serving), moderate fat from deep-frying (~27 g fat per serving), and a significant carbohydrate load from the rice or noodle base and the sweetened drink (~127 g carbohydrate per serving). The sodium content is elevated (~2015 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), driven primarily by the white rice or rice noodle component, and the glycemic load per serving is approximately 20.8, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The saturated fat content from fried fish and processed meatballs is moderate and relevant for cardiovascular disease risk. This set meal is representative of casual Thai dining in Singapore and is commonly ordered as a complete lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite Thai set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of component foods from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. GI is estimated by analogy with white rice and rice noodle GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the variability inherent in restaurant set meals where the specific rice/noodle dish chosen by the customer is not fixed.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimation. All other -1 fields have been replaced with estimated values where defensible component data was available. The set meal composition is variable: the customer chooses their rice or noodle dish, which affects the final macronutrient and GI profile. The nutrient values represent a central estimate assuming a white rice or standard rice noodle base. The sweetened beverage component is assumed to be a standard Thai-style sweetened drink (~230 ml, ~25 g sugar); if a sugar-free or water option is chosen, carbohydrate and sugar values would be lower. Sodium estimate is based on typical Thai restaurant seasoning levels and USDA component data; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. Halal status is listed as false because it cannot be confirmed without restaurant certification; the restaurant may or may not hold halal certification. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (GI 64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant glycaemic driver, moderated by protein and fat from meatballs and fried fish. Glycemic load per serving of 20.8 is calculated using net carbohydrate from solid food components (~32 g) at GI 65.
